content.assessment: International comparison offers limited direct precedent for this proposal because no comparator case in the evidence base modifies admissions mechanics while preserving early tracking. The closest analogy is negative: Bavarian evidence on age-10 selection shows poor predictive validity of the selection instrument itself, which suggests that refining who gets selected (via SES-weighted or coaching-resistant exams) does not address the more fundamental problem that early selection instruments are weak predictors of ability. The cross-national tracking literature (Hanushek & Woessmann) targets the existence of early tracking as the inequality driver, not the fairness of the entry test, so this proposal addresses a different causal lever than the one comparative evidence flags as consequential. Hungary's own distinguishing feature—the entanglement of tracking with Roma segregation—is a contextual factor with no parallel in the German, Austrian, or Finnish tracking debates, meaning transferability of any comparator insight (which mostly concerns non-ethnic SES sorting) is constrained.
opportunities: Could be paired with comparator lessons on curriculum harmonization (as in the Polish and Finnish reforms) to strengthen its ultimately partial reach into structural inequality
strengths: Leaves the tracking structure intact, avoiding the transition-cost risks documented in cross-country structural reforms (e.g., Poland's abrupt reversal)
Targets a mechanism (admission bias) that is at least conceptually adjacent to the within-selection-process fairness question raised by Bavarian predictive-validity evidence
threats: Absent structural change, the between-school variance pattern that places Hungary third-highest in the OECD is likely to persist, since comparator evidence attributes this variance to tracking structure rather than admission procedure alone
weaknesses: No comparator country in the evidence base has attempted admissions-mechanism reform as a standalone lever within an unaltered early-tracking structure, so comparability with tried international mechanisms is weak

Bavarian evidence suggests the selection instrument itself has low predictive validity, implying that adjusting who is selected does not resolve the deeper measurement problem underlying early selection
Does not engage the Hungary-specific ethnic segregation dimension that cross-national comparators (Germany, Austria, Finland) do not share, limiting the relevance of any foreign benchmark

Cross-national tracking research treats the presence of early tracking, not admissions design, as the primary inequality driver, suggesting this proposal is aimed at a secondary rather than primary lever identified by the comparative literature
